coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of biomass-based triple-emission carbon dots for dopamine sensing
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Nie, Zhengpei
Zhong, Wenyu
Liang, Jiaman
Shu, Qunwei
Tan, Kejun
coredata.dc:description: Biomass carbon dots (CDs) typically suffer from red fluorescence quenching in water, limiting their practical applications. This study introduces a simple pH regulation strategy to enhance water-solubility by converting lipophilic surface groups to hydrophilic ones. Using mugwort as a precursor, novel triple-emission biomass-based CDs (T-CDs) with good water-solubility were successfully synthesized. Comprehensive structural and spectral characterizations were performed to elucidate the properties of the T-CDs and the origins of their luminescence, providing valuable insights for the design of future CDs. The T-CDs were further modified with hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), producing modified triple-emission CDs (mT-CDs) with enhanced photostability. These mT-CDs were effectively employed as fluorescent probes for selective dopamine (DA) sensing, achieving a linear range of 0.8 to 300 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.17 μM, demonstrating their potential in biochemical applications. To explore additional applications, mT-CDs were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to create thin films. These films exhibit pink fluorescence under 254 nm UV light and phosphorescence after the UV source is removed, making them suitable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting purposes.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Triple-emission carbon dots
Water-solubility
Photostability
Dopamine
Information encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of DA in human serum
Discussion of the origin of fluorescence
Detection of DA in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A), B) TEM pattern (illustration: high-resolution TEM lattice diagram) and C), D) histogram of the particle size distribution of T-CDs and mT-CDs.
A), E) Survey XPS spectra; high-resolution B), F) C1s; C), G) N1s; D), H) O1s XPS spectra of T-CDs and mT-CDs, respectively.
A), C) UV-VIS, fluorescent excitation and emission spectra; B), D) fluorescent emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths of T-CDs and mT-CDs, respectively.
Changes in fluorescent intensity of T-CDs (black line) and mT-CDs (red line) under 410 nm light radiation for 30 min, A) λ em = 485 nm; B) λ em = 602 nm; C) λ em = 645 nm.
Stability of mT-CDs A) pH; B) strength of salt; C) storage time; fluorescence decay curves of T-CDs D) 420 nm; E) 602 nm; F) 645 nm.

A) Fluorescence spectra of mT-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of DA (0–300 μM); B) the relationship between F 602/F 485 and concentration of DA; C) selectivity; D) anti-interference of mT-CDs.; E) UV-VIS absorption spectra of mT-CDs before and after adding DA, and DA; F) fluorescence decay curves of mT-CDs before and after adding DA.
Synthesis and application diagram of mT-CDs.
Results of DA detection in human serum.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of biomass-based triple-emission carbon dots for dopamine sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescence Analysis and Molecular Sensing, Ministry of Education, Chongqing Key Laboratory of Soft-Matter Material Chemistry and Function Manufacturing, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ziyang Food and Drug Inspection and Testing Center, Ziyang 641399, Sichuan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550003, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, Guizhou Institute of Technology, Guiyang 550003, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biomass carbon dots (CDs) typically suffer from red fluorescence quenching in water, limiting their practical applications. This study introduces a simple pH regulation strategy to enhance water-solubility by converting lipophilic surface groups to hydrophilic ones. Using mugwort as a precursor, novel triple-emission biomass-based CDs (T-CDs) with good water-solubility were successfully synthesized. Comprehensive structural and spectral characterizations were performed to elucidate the properties of the T-CDs and the origins of their luminescence, providing valuable insights for the design of future CDs. The T-CDs were further modified with hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), producing modified triple-emission CDs (mT-CDs) with enhanced photostability. These mT-CDs were effectively employed as fluorescent probes for selective dopamine (DA) sensing, achieving a linear range of 0.8 to 300 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.17 μM, demonstrating their potential in biochemical applications. To explore additional applications, mT-CDs were incorporated into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) to create thin films. These films exhibit pink fluorescence under 254 nm UV light and phosphorescence after the UV source is removed, making them suitable for information encryption and anti-counterfeiting purposes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, biocompatibility, easy functionalization, etc., carbon dots (CDs) [1] have been widely applied in many fields, such as sensing [2,3], biomedicine [4–6], photoelectric devices [7], catalysis [8], anti-counterfeiting and information encryption [9]. Numerous methods for preparing CDs have been reported, such as arc discharge, laser ablation, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods, and microwave techniques. Among these, hydrothermal and solvothermal methods are the simplest and most frequently used.

CDs can be synthesized from various carbon sources, including chemical-based and biomass-based precursors. The former has limitations such as cytotoxicity and high material cost, while the latter is favored by many researchers due to its advantages, such as easy availability, low cost, biocompatibility, and biodegradability. Various biomass sources, such as papaya [10], pomelo peel [11], mulberry leaf [12], waste leather scrap [13], phellodendron chinense schneid [14], black liquor [15] and egg [16] have been used for CDs synthesis. However, early biomass-based CDs were primarily single-emission [17–19], and lacked self-calibration ability, making them susceptible to factors such as background color, local environmental changes, and instrument instability in sensing applications. To overcome these limitations, researchers developed biomass-based multi-emission CDs [20]. The red emission of these multi-emitting CDs typically originates from chlorophyll in leaves. However, due to the hydrophobic nature of the porphyrin structure [12,21,22], leaf-based CDs can achieve red emission only in organic solvents such as ethanol, acetone, and DMF, while their red emission is quenched in aqueous solutions [23,24]. In many application fields of carbon dots, the application of water phase system such as sensing and bio-imaging accounts for a relatively large proportion, so the conversion of lipophilic carbon dots to hydrophilic ones is extremely important. To address the water-solubility issue, Zhao and co-workers synthesized blue and red double-emission CDs via solvent heat treatment of corn bracts, followed by modification with H2N-PEG-NH2 and sodium carbonate, effectively improving the water-solubility of the chlorophyll luminescent center [25]. Cui et al. proposed a method combining solvent extraction with a solvothermal approach to synthesize biomass-derived CDs, maintaining red emission in water, using chlorophyll extracted from pakchoi and polyoxyethylene bisamine (NH2-PEG-NH2) as precursors [26]. Similarly, Wang et al. synthesized dual-emission water-soluble fluorescent CDs using holly leaves and PEI1.8 k as raw materials through the solvothermal method and PEG grafting [27]. These methods all involve the introduction of polymers, making the synthesis process both cumbersome and costly. Therefore, it is essential to explore a simple and low-cost synthesis strategy to enhance the water-solubility of biomass-based multi-emitting CDs. It is worth noting that these methods all introduce more hydrophilic groups to carbon dots to a certain extent, and employ surface modifiers as a bridge to improve the water solubility of red emission. This reveals the feasibility of directly converting lipophilic groups into hydrophilic groups in fluorescence source molecules from the very beginning when investigating the transformation of lipid solubility and water solubility of carbon dots.

The chlorophyll molecule, serving as the source of red fluorescence in carbon dots derived from leaves, contains ester group, carbonyl group and other hydrophilic-prone functional groups. By introducing alkali, it can facilitate the transformation of lipophilic moieties into hydrophilic ones. In this work, novel water-soluble biomass-based three-emission CDs (T-CDs) were synthesized through a one-step solvothermal method using mugwort as a precursor for DA sensing and information encryption, as illustrated in Scheme 1 . Sodium hydroxide was employed to enhance the water-solubility of the red emission of CDs, while CTAB modification was used to improve the photostability of the red emission of leaf-derived CDs. Modified triple-emission CDs (mT-CDs) have good water-solubility and photostability. Based on their interaction with DA, we have developed a ratiometric fluorescence probe for DA detection. Finally, we prepared mT-CDs-PVA films, which exhibit red fluorescence under ultraviolet light (254 nm) and green phosphorescence after the ultraviolet lamp is turned off. These properties were successfully utilized for dual information encryption applications.

An autoclave (polytetrafluoroethylene lining) and a DHG-9036A thermostatic drying oven (Jinghong, Shanghai) were used for CDs synthesis. The fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). The UV-2600 ultraviolet spectrophotometer (Tsushima, Japan) was used for the determination of ultraviolet absorption spectra. The surface morphology and particle size of CDs were characterized using a Talos F200X (Thermo Field, USA) high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) (Bruker, Germany) was used to analyze the functional group categories of CDs. The Thermo ESCALAB 250XI (Thermo Field, USA) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) instrument was used to analyze the surface chemical compositions and chemical states of CDs. A vortex mixer QL-901 (Hainan, China) was used to mix the solutions. The fluorescence lifetimes of the CDs were measured using the FLS 980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrometers (Edin, UK).

Mugwood (from Southwest University Beibei campus, Chongqing), hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), DA hydrochloride, anhydrous ethanol, sodium hydroxide, other organic solvents and salts were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Co., LTD. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were of reagent grade and used without further purification. Ultra-pure water (resistivity of 18.2 MΩ·cm at 25 °C) was used in all experiments.

Fresh mugwort leaves were picked, washed with deionized water, and dried in an oven at 60 °C. The dried leaves were then pulverized using a grinder and set aside. A mixture of 0.5 g of the crushed material and 0.5 g NaOH was added to 7 mL of anhydrous ethanol and subjected to ultrasound for 10 min. This mixture was then transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave and reacted at 160 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the mixture was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature, producing a reddish-brown solution. The solution was centrifuged at 1000 rpm for 10 min and filtered. The filtered supernatant was dialyzed and subsequently stored at 4 °C for future utilization.

The T-CDs solution was diluted tenfold with anhydrous ethanol to obtain solution 1. To produce mT-CDs, solution 1 was mixed with a 0.05 M CTAB aqueous solution at a 7:1 volume ratio and stirred in a water bath at 50 °C for 4 h. The resulting mixture was centrifuged (10000 rpm, 10 min), filtered and dialyzed, and stored at 4 °C for later.

In a BR buffer solution with pH 7, mT-CDs of known volume and concentration were mixed with a DA solution. After incubating at room temperature for 45 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded. The sensitivity of DA detection was analyzed over a concentration range of 0 to 300 μM. Similar methods were used to conduct interference experiments with other small molecules and metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Serum samples were taken from healthy adult women. The supernatant of fresh serum samples, refrigerated overnight, was mixed with acetonitrile at a 1:2 volume ratio to remove macromolecules and proteins, then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was diluted 100 times to minimize matrix effects. The sample is subjected to the standard addition method, where a known concentration of DA is introduced and subsequently detected according to the established procedure. The specific method is as follows: Add 100 μL of mT-CDs and 100 μL BR buffer solution into a 2 mL centrifuge tube, followed by a certain volume of DA solution. The mixture was then diluted to 1 mL with the diluted serum solution. After incubating at room temperature for 45 min, the fluorescence spectra were measured and the data were recorded. The recovery rate and relative standard deviation (RSD) were calculated based on the obtained data.

The solvent-free mT-CDs were added to a 10 % PVA aqueous solution. After thorough mixing, the solution was used to create patterns, which were subsequently dried at 40 °C. Finally, the patterns were irradiated with an ultraviolet lamp to observe the corresponding fluorescent and phosphorescent phenomena.

In this work, mugwort was used as the primary raw material to synthesize biomass CDs (T-CDs) with red emission in water. The optimization of synthesis conditions, shown in Fig. S1, indicated that the optimal conditions are 0.5 g of dry mugwort, 0.5 g of sodium hydroxide, at 160 °C for 4 h. To investigate the improvement in the water-solubility of the red emission, we conducted a series of experiments. Initially, mugwort CDs were synthesized using different solvents without alkali. As shown in Fig. S2, except for those synthesized via hydrothermal methods, all CDs synthesized in other organic solvents exhibited red emission in the solvent, but this red emission was quenched in water, showing only blue emission-consistent with previous reports [24,28,29]. The fluorescence spectrum of the ethanol-dispersed mugwort solution (Fig. S2 B) displayed a strong red emission at 677.5 nm, which is attributed to chlorophyll [22,23]. Further analysis of chlorophyll's structure revealed that its large porphyrin ring and long alkyl chain contribute to its hydrophobicity. To improve its water-solubility, we focused on its functional groups. As shown in Fig. S3 A, chlorophyll's molecular structure contains two ester groups, which we hypothesized could be converted to carboxylic groups to enhance water-solubility. This led us to experiment with adding acid or base. The results, as depicted in Fig. S2 C, indicate that the addition of acid does not enhance the water-solubility of the red emission, likely due to the instability of magnesium in the porphyrin ring under acidic conditions. Under such conditions, magnesium is easily replaced by hydrogen ions, which subsequently leads to the decomposition of the porphyrin ring, thus eliminating the red fluorescence. Conversely, adding a strong base proved effective (Fig. S2 D). After adding a base, red emission was observed in aqueous solution, although the red peak band exhibited an acromion, and the main emission peak significantly blue-shifted. Additionally, it was noted that stronger base favored the red emission of mugwort-based CDs in aqueous solution. With sodium hydroxide and potassium hydroxide, the CDs showed strong red double emission in aqueous solution, while weaker bases resulted in diminished or absent red emission. Thus, alkali was a critical factor in enhancing the water-solubility of red emissions in this study. The specific idea for the synthesis of T-CDs is presented in Scheme. 2 . In addition, the presence of a chlorophyll-derived structure in the T-CDs mass spectrum (Fig. S3 B) further confirmed this conclusion [30]. Since chlorophyll is widely present in green plants as the key component in photosynthesis, we tested the universality of this synthesis method by using different leaves as raw materials, and providing strong alkaline conditions with sodium hydroxide. The fluorescence spectra of CDs synthesized from these leaves were similar to those from mugwort (Fig. S3 C), confirming the method's feasibility and applicability.

The morphology and structure of CDs were analyzed using TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A-B, the CDs are well dispersed both before and after modification. The average particle size of the mT-CDs (Fig. 1 D) is approximately 6.1 nm, which is about 1.6 times larger than the T-CDs (Fig. 1 C), indicating the successful modification of CTAB on the surface of the T-CDs [31]. Additionally, the HR-TEM images (insets at the top-left of Fig. 1 A-B) reveal that both T-CDs and mT-CDs exhibit clear lattice fringes with lattice spacings of 0.33 nm and 0.32 nm, respectively, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphene [32,33].

To analyze their functional groups and surface components, the FTIR spectra of the CDs were measured, as shown in Fig. S4. The spectra reveal that the CDs exhibit nearly identical absorption peaks before and after modification. The broad absorption about 3400 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH bonds. The peaks observed at approximately 2900, 2850, 1630, 1570, 1460, 1410, 1070 and 600–700 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CH, CO, C=C/C-N, CN, CO, and the bending vibration of CH, respectively [22,23,34–39]. However, the mT-CDs exhibit two additional peaks at 910 and 964 cm−1, likely associated with the presence of Br from the CTAB modifier [40], confirming the successful modification of the T-CDs.

XPS spectra were also used to characterize the chemical components of T-CDs and mT-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 A, the survey XPS spectrum of T-CDs displays three characteristic peaks at 285.07, 402.14 and 531.21 eV, corresponding to the main components: C (78.90 % atomic percentage), N (1.87 % atomic percentage) and O (19.23 % atomic percentage), respectively. In comparison, mT-CDs show the same primary elements but with different content: C (86.86 % atomic percentage), N (3.28 % atomic percentage), and O (9.86 % atomic percentage). The significant increase in C and N content, along with a decrease in O content, further confirms the successful modification of the T-CDs with CTAB, which is rich in C and N. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of T-CDs (Fig. 2 B) reveals three types of carbon bonds: C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O, and C=N/C=O, which are also present in mT-CDs (Fig. 2 F). The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2 C) shows three peaks at 397.58, 398.83 and 400.03 eV, corresponding to pyrrole N, pyridine N and graphite N, respectively. The mT-CDs (Fig. 2 G) exhibit a notable increase in graphitic N content, which, when combined with the structure of CTAB, confirms the successful modification of T-CDs. Additionally, the O1s spectra of both T-CDs and mT-CDs show peaks corresponding to CO and CO bonds [22].

To study the optical properties of CDs, UV–vis and fluorescence spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 3 . The UV–vis spectra of the CDs before and after modification are nearly identical, with three major absorption peaks at 269, 278 and 408 nm. These peaks correspond to the π → π* transition of CC [21,38], and the n → π* transition of CO and CN [41], respectively. The excitation and emission spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 3 A and C. Within the 290–430 nm range, both T-CDs and mT-CDs show similar shifts in emission wavelengths with varying excitation wavelengths. Specifically, as the excitation wavelength redshifts, the blue emission peak also redshifts, while the two red peaks only change in intensity but remain fixed in positions (Fig. 3 B and D).

To assess the photostability of the CDs, we conducted a continuous fluorescence scan under 410 nm excitation for 1800 s. The results showed that the blue peak of T-CDs presented excellent photostability, instead, the two red peaks were less stable. To address this, T-CDs were modified by CTAB, and the modification conditions were optimized, as shown in Fig. S5. After modification, the photostability of the 602 nm red emission peak was significantly improved (Fig. 4B), providing a solid foundation for further applications.

According to some literature [42,43], quinine sulfate and rhodamine B were used as the standard materials to measure the relative fluorescence quantum yields (QY) of CDs before and after modification. The results indicated that the surface modification with CTAB had little impact on the QY of the CDs (Fig. S6), serving primarily to stabilize the red emission center. However, despite the improvement in the photostability of the 645 nm fluorescence peak, its intensity decreased after modification and requires further enhancement, as shown in Fig. 4C. Nevertheless, this issue did not affect the application of mT-CDs in ratiometric fluorescence detection.

The exploration of fluorescence behavior of carbon dots in different solvents holds significant value. In this work, T-CDs and mT-CDs were dissolved in solvents of different polarity (water, formamide, dimethyl sulfoxide, N, N-dimethylformamide, ethanol, n-butanol, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane, cyclohexane, petroleum ether), and next fluorescence tests were carried out. As shown in Fig. S7 and S8, T-CDs in most of the selected solvents can show triple-emission fluorescence, and the location of the emission peak is basically not affected by the solvent, except for the difference in intensity. In Fig. S9 and S10, the fluorescence performance of mT-CDs is basically the same as that of T-CDs. However, due to the difference in dilution ratio, the intensity of blue fluorescence peak is significantly different from that of red fluorescence peak of mT-CDs (causing the red fluorescence peak in the figure is not obvious compared with T-CDs).

We studied the stability of the CDs under various experimental conditions, including different pH levels, salt concentrations, and storage times, using fluorescence tests. As shown in Fig. 5 A and S11, the effect of pH on both T-CDs and mT-CDs was negligible. However, for T-CDs, the blue peak remained almost unchanged, the intensity of the two red peaks gradually decreased as the salt concentration increased. In contrast, the three peaks of mT-CDs showed minimal changes and were almost unaffected by salt concentration. After four and a half months of storage (Fig. 5 C), the red fluorescence intensities at 602 nm and 645 nm for T-CDs significantly decreased, whereas mT-CDs exhibited only minor changes, demonstrating that surface modification played a crucial role in improving the stability of red emission in water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discussion of the origin of fluorescence
ce:section.ce:para: After analyzing the optical properties of T-CDs and mT-CDs, we found that the CTAB modification only stabilized the fluorescence emission center and had almost no effect on the position and shape of the fluorescence peak. Therefore, the source of fluorescence emission of mT-CDs did not change, so we only need to explore the source of fluorescence emission of T-CDs. To summarize, the blue fluorescence emission of the CDs shows prominent stability, unaffected by external factors such as pH, salt concentration, light radiation and storage time. This stability aligns with the luminescence of the carbon core state. However, the excitation-dependent property may be attributed to the varying surface states formed by differing degrees of oxidation and carbonization, influenced by the combined effects of sodium hydroxide and ethanol [44–46]. Fig. 5 DF shows the fluorescence decay curves of the three peaks of T-CDs. After fitting the curves with a second-order exponential function, the fluorescence lifetimes were determined to be 2.387 ns, 8.962 ns and 7.195 ns, respectively. Interestingly, the fitting results (Table. S1) indicate that the 420 nm peak contains τ1 components related to the carbon nucleus and τ2 components related to the surface defect states, accounting for 29.82 % and 70.18 % of the total lifetime, respectively. This suggests that the radiation transition at this wavelength is due to the synergistic effect of the carbon core structure and surface defect states [47]. In contrast, the 602 nm and 645 nm peaks, though also contain τ1 and τ2 components after exponential fitting, are sensitive to factors such as salt concentration, light radiation and storage time. Both peaks are excitation-independent, suggesting that the composition of their fluorescence emission centers is similar to each other but different from that of the blue peak. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of these red peaks was reduced after dialysis (Fig. S11 C). The characteristic Gaussian peaks in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3 A) and the peaks corresponding to a chlorophyll-derived structure in mass spectrometry (Fig. S3 B) suggest that the emissions at 602 nm and 645 nm mainly originate from a molecule state [48,49]. The similar fluorescence spectra of CDs synthesized from different chlorophyll-containing leaves (Fig. S3 C) further support this conclusion. Based on the analysis of UV-VIS and XPS spectra, T-CDs are rich in oxygen-containing groups such as CO and CO, whose strong electron-withdrawing abilities can narrow the HUMO-LUMO band gap, enhancing radiation transitions. Thus, a weaker component of the red luminescence center is likely attributable to the sp2 domain with oxygen-containing groups (surface state) [50]. Therefore, it is speculated that the two red peaks result from a combination of the molecule state inherited from the porphyrin structure and surface state.

Based on the optical properties and stability studies, mT-CDs are suitable as ratio-fluorescence probes for detecting molecules and ions. To demonstrate their practical potential, mT-CDs were applied to DA sensing. DA significantly quenched the 602 nm fluorescence peak of mT-CDs, while the 485 nm fluorescence peak remained largely unaffected. Based on this observation, we established a ratio fluorescence analysis method for DA detection. To optimize the detection conditions, the ratio F/F′ (where F and F′ represent the ratio of fluorescent intensity of the 602 nm emission to 485 nm emission of mT-CDs before and after the addition of DA, respectively) was used as an evaluation index. Various conditions were testes, including buffer type, pH, salt concentration (ionic strength), incubation time, and detection temperature. The optimal detection conditions were found to be a BR buffer at pH 7, no added salt, 45 min of incubation, and detection at room temperature (Fig. S12).

Under the optimal detecting conditions, as the DA concentration increased, the 602 nm fluorescence peak of mT-CDs was significantly quenched, while the fluorescent intensity of the 485 nm peak also showed a slightly decrease. The fluorescence spectra of mT-CDs were measured at different concentrations of DA, as shown in Fig. 6 A. A linear relationship between F 602 /F 485 and DA concentration was established (Fig. 6B). As the DA concentration increased from 0.8 μM to 300 μM, the linear relationship could be fitted with the equation y = 7.34456–0.01949×, R 2 = 0.9901. Using the 3δ rule, the LOD was calculated to be 0.17 μM (n = 7). A rough comparison with other fluorescent probes indicates that this method is relatively effective for DA detection, with specific comparative data provided in Table S2.

To assess the selectivity of mT-CDs for DA sensing, several small molecules and ions were individually added to mT-CDs, and their responses were determined by measuring the corresponding fluorescence spectra. The tested substances included dopamine (DA), glutamic acid (Glu), ascorbic acid (AA), aspartic acid (Asn), glycine (Gly), uric acid (UA), glucose (Glc), histidine (His), arginine (Arg), cysteine (Cys), leucine (Leu), tyrosine (Tyr), tryptophan (Trp), and various ions (Zn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Al3+, Na+, K+, SO4 2−, ClO−, HCO3 −, CO3 2−, Cl−, NO3 −, SCN−). Except for DA at a concentration of 100 μM and SCN− at 250 μM, all other molecules and ions were tested at 500 μM. As can be seen from Fig. 6 C, the fluorescence signal ratio of mT-CDs remained nearly unchanged when other molecules or ions, at much higher concentrations than DA, were introduced. In contrast, the addition of DA caused a significant change, demonstrating that mT-CDs have excellent selectivity for DA sensing. Furthermore, interference experiments were conducted to evaluate the anti-interference ability of mT-CDs as a fluorescent probe. DA was mixed with other molecules or ions, respectively, and then mT-CDs were added for fluorescence sensing. As shown in Fig. 6 D, the signal was almost undisturbed by the presence of other substances, confirming that mT-CDs possess strong selectivity and anti-interference ability for DA detection.

Based on UV-VIS absorption spectra and fluorescence decay curves, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching of mT-CDs at 602 nm was investigated briefly. As shown in Fig. 6 E, the absorption spectrum of DA shows no absorption in the range of 400–700 nm, suggesting that there is no energy transfer between DA and mT-CDs. This rules out fluorescence quenching caused by the inner filter effect (IFE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [51–54]. After the addition of DA, the absorption spectrum of the mT-CDs/ DA mixture closely matches the sum of the individual absorption spectra of mT-CDs and DA. This indicates that the absorption position of mT-CDs remains unaffected, with only an increase in absorption intensity after addition of DA, suggesting that no new substance is generated during the process. Additionally, the fluorescence lifetimes of mT-CDs before and after the addition of DA were compared (Fig. 6 F), revealing a decrease in fluorescence lifetime from 7.364 ns to 7.040 ns (The specific fluorescence lifetime compositions are shown in Table S3). Although the fluorescence lifetime changes, but the change is small, on the contrary, the fluorescence peak of 602 nm quenching degree is significant. At this point, it seems unreasonable to attribute it solely to dynamic quenching. Theoretically, the type of quenching mechanism can be described by Stern-Volmer equation.
F 0
/ F = 1 +
K sv
Q

(F
0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of the fluorophore in the absence and presence of the quencher respectively, [Q] represents the concentration of the quencher and K
sv represents the quenching constant.)

In this work, as shown in Fig. S13, the relationship between F 0/F and DA concentration is not simple linear, but a positive deviation from linearity in the SV plot is obtained (
F 0
/ F = 0.9874 + 0.006240
Q
+ 3.294 ∗
10
− 5
Q
2
, R
2 = 0.9974), which means that this quenching process is not a complete dynamic quenching or a complete static quenching, but a combination of the two. [55] In summary, the fluorescence quenching of mT-CDs-DA system may be mainly attributed to the synergistic effect of dynamic quenching and static quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of DA in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the practicability of the probe, human serum was used for detection. As shown in Table 1 , the recoveries ranged from 87.53 % to 104.0 %, with a relative standard deviation of less than 5.32 %. These results indicate that mT-CDs perform well in the recovery detection of human serum, demonstrating good potential for practical applications.

Information security is a critical topic, and researchers continually seek efficient and environmentally friendly anti-counterfeiting materials. To demonstrate the potential application of the prepared-mT-CDs in anticounterfeiting, we mixed the biomass-based CDs (mT-CDs) with a PVA aqueous solution, drew pre-designed patterns, and dried them to create an mT-CDs/PVA composite film. As shown in Fig. S14, under a 254 nm UV lamp, the “12” in “1234” displays pink fluorescence, and “OK” in “OKNO” shows the same effect. Notably, after turning off the UV lamp, both “12” and “OK” emit turquoise phosphorescence, and the sustainable phosphorescence time is about 0.85–1 s, demonstrating the dual anti-counterfeiting capability of mT-CDs. These experimental results indicate that the prepared mT-CDs have significant potential for applications in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting, offering a new avenue for the development of biomass-based CDs.

In this work, biomass-based T-CDs with multiple emissions were successfully synthesized using the solvothermal method. The introduction of sodium hydroxide enhanced the stability of red fluorescent CDs in water, broadening their applicability in aqueous environments. Additionally, T-CDs were modified with CTAB to improve their photostability. The luminescence mechanism study indicates that the blue emission results from the synergistic interaction between the carbon core state and surface defect states, while the red emissions are primarily due to the molecular and surface states. Leveraging the strong signal response to DA, a ratiometric fluorescence probe was developed for the selective detection of DA. Moreover, the prepared CDs show significant potential in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting applications.

Zhengpei Nie: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Wenyu Zhong: Validation, Data curation. Jiaman Liang: Writing – review & editing. Qunwei Shu: Supervision, Project administration. Kejun Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111726.
